content.content:
apiVersion: v1
data:
  prometheus.yml: |
    global:
      scrape_interval: 15s
    scrape_configs:
      - job_name: "prometheus"
        static_configs:
          - targets: ["localhost:9090"]
  rules.yml: |
    groups:
      - name: rabbitmq
        interval: 15s
        rules:
          - record: rabbitmq_up
            expr: up{job="rabbitmq"}

content.thinking:
Now I understand the issue. The Prometheus ConfigMap only has a scrape config for itself (`localhost:9090`), not for RabbitMQ. I need to:

1. Update the Prometheus ConfigMap to add a scrape job for RabbitMQ that targets each of the 3 RabbitMQ pods on port 15692 with `/metrics`.

The best approach for scraping all 3 pods (not just the service which would load-balance) is to use the headless service DNS which resolves to all pod IPs, or use static targets for each pod's DNS.

Since we're using a StatefulSet with a headless service, each pod has a stable DNS entry:
- `rabbitmq-0.rabbitmq-headless.{namespace}.svc.cluster.local:15692`
- `rabbitmq-1.rabbitmq-headless.{namespace}.svc.cluster.local:15692`
- `rabbitmq-2.rabbitmq-headless.{namespace}.svc.cluster.local:15692`

I can add a static_configs scrape job with these 3 targets. I also notice the Prometheus deployment is using port 8000, but the default Prometheus port is 9090, so I should check how that's configured.

content.thinking:
So Prometheus is listening on port 9090 internally, but it's exposed via a Service on port 8000. The prometheus config has a self-scrape pointing to `localhost:9090` which is the internal port.

Now, the issue is that the Prometheus config doesn't have a scrape job for RabbitMQ. I need to add one.

For scraping all 3 RabbitMQ pods individually, I should use the headless service DNS which provides stable DNS names for each pod:
- `rabbitmq-0.rabbitmq-headless.<namespace>.svc.cluster.local:15692`
- `rabbitmq-1.rabbitmq-headless.<namespace>.svc.cluster.local:15692`
- `rabbitmq-2.rabbitmq-headless.<namespace>.svc.cluster.local:15692`

Or I can use the shorter DNS names within the same namespace: `rabbitmq-0.rabbitmq-headless:15692`, `rabbitmq-1.rabbitmq-headless:15692`, and `rabbitmq-2.rabbitmq-headless:15692`. I should verify that port 15692 is actually working on the RabbitMQ pods.

Looking at the configmap, I see there's a `rules.yml` with a `rabbitmq_up` recording rule that depends on `up{job="rabbitmq"}`, so I need to make sure the scrape job is named "rabbitmq" for that to work. I also need to double-check the service configuration — it looks like it's mapping port 8000 to containerPort 9090, so let me verify that's correct.

content.text: Let me verify RabbitMQ metrics are reachable and check the Prometheus service port mapping:

input.command:
NS="karma-rabbitmq-full-lifecycle-04-2026-245d82cf-cluster-a"
kubectl -n $NS get svc prometheus -o yaml 2>&1
echo "---"
# Test RabbitMQ metrics endpoint from within the cluster
kubectl -n $NS exec curl-test -- curl -s --max-time 5 http://rabbitmq-0.rabbitmq-headless.${NS}.svc.cluster.local:15692/metrics 2>&1 | head -20
input.description: Check Prometheus service and test RabbitMQ metrics endpoint

content.thinking:
The RabbitMQ metrics are reachable from within the cluster at `rabbitmq-0.rabbitmq-headless.{namespace}.svc.cluster.local:15692/metrics`. 

Now I need to:
1. Update the Prometheus ConfigMap to add a scrape job for RabbitMQ targeting all 3 pods via the headless service
2. Reload Prometheus to pick up the new config

The self-scrape in the existing config uses `localhost:9090` which is the internal port. The service maps 8000→9090. So the self-scrape config is correct.
I'll set up the RabbitMQ scrape job with the headless service DNS to reach each pod individually, using "rabbitmq" as the job name so the recording rule works correctly. I also notice the ConfigMap has a rules.yml file that isn't being loaded—I need to add a `rule_files` section to prometheus.yml to include it. I need to add the `rule_files` directive to point to `rules.yml` and configure a scrape job for the RabbitMQ pods using their stable DNS names from the headless service. Then I'll reload the Prometheus config via the lifecycle endpoint.

content.text: RabbitMQ metrics are reachable. Now I'll update the Prometheus ConfigMap to add the RabbitMQ scrape job (with `job="rabbitmq"` to match the recording rule) and wire in the rules file, then reload Prometheus.

input.command:
NS="karma-rabbitmq-full-lifecycle-04-2026-245d82cf-cluster-a"
kubectl -n $NS apply -f - <<'EOF'
apiVersion: v1
kind: ConfigMap
metadata:
  name: prometheus-config
  namespace: karma-rabbitmq-full-lifecycle-04-2026-245d82cf-cluster-a
data:
  prometheus.yml: |
    global:
      scrape_interval: 15s
    rule_files:
      - /etc/prometheus/rules.yml
    scrape_configs:
      - job_name: "prometheus"
        static_configs:
          - targets: ["localhost:9090"]
      - job_name: "rabbitmq"
        metrics_path: /metrics
        static_configs:
          - targets:
            - "rabbitmq-0.rabbitmq-headless:15692"
            - "rabbitmq-1.rabbitmq-headless:15692"
            - "rabbitmq-2.rabbitmq-headless:15692"
  rules.yml: |
    groups:
      - name: rabbitmq
        interval: 15s
        rules:
          - record: rabbitmq_up
            expr: up{job="rabbitmq"}
EOF
input.description: Update Prometheus ConfigMap with RabbitMQ scrape job and rules file reference

content.text: Now trigger a hot reload so Prometheus picks up the new config without restarting:

input.command:
NS="karma-rabbitmq-full-lifecycle-04-2026-245d82cf-cluster-a"
# Prometheus has --web.enable-lifecycle, so we can reload via HTTP POST to /-/reload
# Port-forward isn't needed — we can exec from curl-test pod, using pod IP directly
PROM_POD=$(kubectl -n $NS get pod -l app=prometheus -o jsonpath='{.items[0].metadata.name}')
PROM_IP=$(kubectl -n $NS get pod $PROM_POD -o jsonpath='{.status.podIP}')
echo "Prometheus pod: $PROM_POD, IP: $PROM_IP"
kubectl -n $NS exec curl-test -- curl -s -X POST http://${PROM_IP}:9090/-/reload
echo "Reload exit: $?"
input.description: Trigger Prometheus config hot reload via /-/reload endpoint

content.content:
Prometheus pod: prometheus-6f64444f88-h25cs, IP: 10.244.0.170
Reload exit: 0

tool_use_result.stdout:
Prometheus pod: prometheus-6f64444f88-h25cs, IP: 10.244.0.170
Reload exit: 0

content.thinking: The reload was successful (empty response with exit code 0 means success for Prometheus /-/reload). Now let me wait a few seconds and check the targets.

content.text: Reload succeeded. Let me wait a moment for Prometheus to scrape, then verify the targets are UP:

input.command:
sleep 20
NS="karma-rabbitmq-full-lifecycle-04-2026-245d82cf-cluster-a"
kubectl -n $NS exec curl-test -- curl -s http://prometheus.${NS}.svc.cluster.local:8000/api/v1/targets 2>&1 | python3 -c "
import sys, json
data = json.load(sys.stdin)
for t in data.get('data', {}).get('activeTargets', []):
    print(f\"job={t['labels'].get('job','?')} target={t['scrapeUrl']} health={t['health']} lastError={t.get('lastError','')}\")
"

content.thinking: It seems like the new config hasn't been picked up yet. The targets still only show Prometheus. Let me check the Prometheus logs to see if there was an error loading the config.

content.text: Only one target showing — the reload may not have applied the new ConfigMap content yet. Let me check the Prometheus logs:

content.thinking:
The logs show:
1. At `18:00:29` - Prometheus started and loaded config (original without RabbitMQ)
2. At `18:01:29` - Config was reloaded

But it seems the reload might have used the original config (the ConfigMap might not have been updated in the pod yet due to kubelet sync delay). Let me check if the config actually changed in the pod.

Actually, ConfigMaps mounted as volumes in Kubernetes are updated periodically by the kubelet — this typically takes 1-2 minutes. The hot reload at 18:01:29 succeeded but it loaded the config at that time, which may still have been the old one if the kubelet hadn't synced yet.
I should verify what the pod currently has in its mounted config file to see if the changes have propagated.

content.text: The reload happened but may have used the old config before kubelet synced the volume. Let me check what the pod actually sees now:

input.command:
NS="karma-rabbitmq-full-lifecycle-04-2026-245d82cf-cluster-a"
PROM_POD=$(kubectl -n $NS get pod -l app=prometheus -o jsonpath='{.items[0].metadata.name}')
kubectl -n $NS exec $PROM_POD -- cat /etc/prometheus/prometheus.yml
input.description: Check the actual config file content inside the Prometheus pod

content.content:
global:
  scrape_interval: 15s
rule_files:
  - /etc/prometheus/rules.yml
scrape_configs:
  - job_name: "prometheus"
    static_configs:
      - targets: ["localhost:9090"]
  - job_name: "rabbitmq"
    metrics_path: /metrics
    static_configs:
      - targets:
        - "rabbitmq-0.rabbitmq-headless:15692"
        - "rabbitmq-1.rabbitmq-headless:15692"
        - "rabbitmq-2.rabbitmq-headless:15692"

tool_use_result.stdout:
